---
title: "Pulse Disturbances from Hurricanes Differentially Structure α- and β-Diversity in Coastal Benthic Communities"
#format: html
format: docx 
author:
  - name: "Craig R. McClain"
    affiliation: "Dept. of Biology, University of Louisiana at Lafayette"
    email: "craig.mcclain@louisiana.edu"
  - name: "Jacob Badcock"
    affiliation: "Dept. of Biology, University of Louisiana at Lafayette"
  - name: "Samuel Copley"
    affiliation: "Dept. of Biology, University of Louisiana at Lafayette"
  - name: "Sydney McDermott"
    affiliation: "Dept. of Biology, University of Louisiana at Lafayette"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: ecology.csl
engine: knitr
prefer-html: true
---

```{r load-packages, include=FALSE}
#### 1. Load Required Packages with Annotations ####

if (!require("pacman")) install.packages("pacman")  # For convenient package loading
pacman::p_load(
  
  # Data manipulation
  dplyr,       # Data wrangling: filter, mutate, summarize, joins
  tibble,      # Modern data frames
  tidyr,       # Pivoting data (long <-> wide), separate columns
  stringr,     # String manipulation (str_extract, str_trim)
  lubridate,   # Date handling (as.POSIXct, year/month extraction)
  
  # Statistics / Modeling
  mgcv,        # GAMs / GAMMs
  nlme,        # Random effects for GAMMs
  vegan,       # TBI and other diversity metrics
  hillR,       # Hill numbers for alpha diversity
  adespatial,  # Temporal Beta-diversity Index (TBI)
  betapart,    # Beta-diversity calculations
  mgcv,        # Partial R2
  
  # Visualization
  ggplot2,     # Plotting
  ggrepel,     # Text labels in ggplot without overlap
  ggpubr,      # Arranging multiple ggplots (ggarrange)
  gridExtra,   # Arranging plots (grid.arrange)
  scales,      # Axis formatting in ggplot
  grid,        # For low-level plot adjustments
  geomtextpath, # Plotting hurricane lines and labels
  
  # GAM diagnostics / plotting
  gratia,      # GAM plotting, checking, and residual diagnostics
  
  # Reporting / tables
  knitr,       # kable for tables
  
  # Tidying model outputs
  broom,        # Tidy model outputs into data frames
  
  #Webshot needed to render html tables
  webshot,
  webshot2
)

```

```{r}
#| label: Load Data & Clean
#| include: false

#### 2. Load Data ####

setwd("~/Dropbox/Terrebonne/Data/TB2B Core Data")
  
  core       <- read.csv("TB2B_CoreData.csv")
  species    <- read.csv("TB2B_Taxa.csv")
  core_spec  <- read.csv("TB2B_CoreByTaxa_With_Juv.csv")
  
#### 3. Data Cleaning & Preparation ####

  # 3.1 Replace NA with 0 in species and core datasets
      core_spec[is.na(core_spec)] <- 0
      core[is.na(core)] <- 0
  
  # 3.2 Filter out rows with no species counted in core_spec (ignoring Sample.ID)
      core_spec <- core_spec %>%
        filter(rowSums(select(., -1)) > 0)
  
  # 3.3 Remove 2024 samples from core and filter rows with species counts > 0
      #core <- core %>% 
      #  slice(-c(361:407)) %>%
      #  filter(rowSums(select(., -(1:16), -(31:38))) > 0)
  
  # 3.4 Filter cores with Core.Quality > 8
      core <- core %>% filter(Core.Quality > 8)
  
  # 3.5 Synchronize core_spec with filtered core by Sample.ID & Core.Quality
      core_spec <- core_spec %>%
      inner_join(core %>% select(Sample.ID, Core.Quality), by = "Sample.ID") %>%
      filter(Core.Quality > 8) %>%
      select(-Core.Quality)
  
  # 3.6 Convert Collection.Date to POSIXct
      core <- core %>%
        mutate(Collection.Date =  as.Date(Collection.Date, format = "%m/%d/%Y"),
               Collection.Date2 = as.POSIXct(Collection.Date, format = "%m/%d/%Y"),
               Core.Quality = factor(Core.Quality, levels = c("10", "9", "8", "7")),
               Months_Since_Hurricane = factor(Months_Since_Hurricane, levels = c("Pre", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5")))
  
  # 3.7 Combine Adult + Juvenile counts for species, remove juvenile columns
        species_bases <- c("Biv.1", "Biv.2", "Biv.3", "Biv.6", "Biv.7", "Biv.9", "Biv.12", "Biv.13",
          "Biv.15", "Biv.17", "Biv.18", "Gas.1", "Gas.2", "Gas.3", "Gas.4", "Gas.5",
          "Gas.6", "Gas.7", "Gas.9", "Gas.10", "Gas.11", "Oph.1", "Oph.2", "Oph.3")
  
        core_spec_new <- core_spec %>%
          mutate(across(all_of(species_bases), ~ . + get(paste0(cur_column(), ".juv.")))) %>%
          select(-matches("\\.juv\\."))
```

## Abstract

Coastal benthic communities are shaped by a dynamic interplay between pulse disturbances, such as hurricanes, and press stressors, including ongoing environmental variability. We examined a five‑year time series of a benthic community and environmental data from Terrebonne Bay, Louisiana, spanning five major hurricanes. Repeated, high‑intensity hurricanes produced abrupt, short‑term declines in α‑diversity (species richness and Hill numbers), particularly within the first one to three months post‑disturbance. In contrast, temporal β‑diversity exhibited more gradual and context‑dependent responses, with pulses of turnover driven by species losses immediately post‑storm and by gains in subsequent months. After accounting for environmental covariates, hurricane effects on β‑diversity diminished, whereas α‑diversity remained strongly depressed, indicating that compositional shifts are more strongly modulated by background and disturbance-induced environmental changes. Differences in storm intensity, duration, and directness of impact further shaped these responses. Our results highlight that disturbance effects on α‑ and β‑diversity are decoupled in time and mechanism: pulse events rapidly reset richness while longer‑term press drivers and disturbance recovery govern community reorganization. Integrating pulse and press perspectives improves predictions of resilience, recovery, and long‑term stability in coastal ecosystems facing increasing extreme weather under climate change.

## Introduction

The structure of ecological communities is in constant temporal flux. These changes can manifest as shifts in species richness (α-diversity) and changes in community composition that may, when they alter differences among communities, be reflected in β-diversity. However, the relative temporal dynamics of α- and β-diversity remain poorly understood, including whether they respond simultaneously or asynchronously, or if one is consistently more volatile than the other [@dornelas2023; @blowes2019; @lindholm2021]. Recent research indicates that even when α-diversity remains stable, species identities may still be reshuffled, implying that relying on richness alone can mask significant biodiversity change [@dornelas2023b; @blowes2019]. Consequently, some authors [@magurran2019] have argued that trends in richness are insufficient for understanding temporal changes in diversity. Instead, temporal β-diversity should be the primary focus, especially given increasing evidence of local compositional changes in the Anthropocene [@dornelas2023b]. This perspective reflects a broader recognition that univariate metrics, such as species richness, often fail to capture community reorganization [@gotelli2001; @hillebrand2017; @dornelas2014]. Supporting this view, recent efforts including the IPBES-IPCC workshop on “Biodiversity and Climate Change” have emphasized the need to understand and monitor changes in community composition under climate change [@pörtner2021].

Considering α- and β-diversity jointly provides a more comprehensive understanding of the ecological mechanisms underlying community change than examining either metric in isolation. If temporal β-diversity changes but α-diversity does not, i.e. species identities shuffle while richness is maintained, coexistence and diversity-dependent feedback mechanisms remain even as habitat filtering alters which niches are occupied [@baselga2015; @picoche2019; @nakadai2020; @magurran2010]. For example, even when α-diversity remains constant, the loss of long-lived or functionally important species and their replacement by smaller, short-lived ones can substantially alter community composition and ecosystem functioning [@lotze2006; @ogorman2011; @oliver2015]. Conversely, temporal α-diversity can change while β-diversity remains unchanged, reflecting a step wise reduction in richness due to the consistent loss of the same group of species through time. This pattern may arise through several non-exclusive mechanisms. [@germain2018; @kraft2014]. Stronger habitat filtering can exclude species whose physiological tolerances or functional traits no longer match prevailing environmental conditions, reducing the set of species able to persist locally. Changes in environmental context or resource availability can also weaken stabilizing coexistence mechanisms allowing competitively dominant species to exclude others thereby reducing diversity. These processes can also interact: intensified environmental filtering can simultaneously narrow niche space and disrupt coexistence dynamics, producing compounded effects on community structure and diversity.

Even in the absence of major disturbances, communities experience continuous baseline fluctuations due to ecological drift, demographic stochasticity, and small-scale environmental heterogeneity [@gilbert2017; @adler2007; @fung2023], which can lead to temporal changes in α- and β-diversity. Disturbances change communities beyond these baseline fluctuations. Press disturbances, such as climate change, can gradually and consistently alter environmental conditions, exerting long-term pressure on biodiversity [@collins2020; @wang2023; @hu2021]. In contrast, pulse disturbances, such as hurricanes or wildfires, act over short timescales but can induce immediate and substantial shifts in ecological communities, often exceeding the magnitude of natural background fluctuations [@gutiérrez-fonseca2023; @xi2018; @strand2019]. Understanding how communities respond to such discrete, high-intensity events is essential for predicting ecological resilience and recovery especially as the frequency of such events increases due to climate change [@zampieri2025; @campbell2022; @walker2025; @polazzo2024]. Yet, the mechanisms driving post-disturbance changes in α- and β-diversity remain poorly characterized, particularly in marine environments [@mori2018; @myers2015; @tatsumi2020]. Here, we focus on hurricanes as a model pulse disturbance, alongside ongoing press disturbances of coastal land loss, environmental degradation, and climate change, to examine how repeated and sustained environmental changes reshape the marine community of an estuarine bay over time.

Hurricanes cause abrupt and often substantial ecological impacts in coastal ecosystems [@greening2006]. Unlike other ecological disturbances, such as grassland and forest fires, tree falls, or coral bleaching events, hurricanes are frequently studied in isolation rather than as repeated, ecologically predictable events in regions subject to increased hurricane activity [@greening2006]. In the southeastern United States, for example, hurricane frequency has increased since 1996 and is projected to rise further in the coming decades [@emanuel2005; @goldenberg2001; @webster2005]. Notably, hurricanes are now more frequently forming before the official Atlantic season (June 1–November 30), with early-season landfalls and higher-intensity storms becoming more common [@collins2017; @truchelut2022]. The ecological consequences of hurricanes are highly variable, influenced by storm surge, wind intensity, and sediment redistribution [@lugo2008; @schoener2003; @smith2009]. Importantly, storm magnitude alone does not always predict ecological effect: even moderate hurricanes (category 1–3) can induce extensive mortality and reshuffle community composition in areas that have not experienced recent storms [@paerl2001; @mumby2011; @lin2020]. Examining hurricanes as repeated, high-intensity pulse disturbances provides a unique opportunity to understand how abrupt and recurrent environmental perturbations reshape ecological community richness, composition, and overall structure.

Building on this perspective, we examine a five-year dataset of benthic sampling in Terrebonne–Timbalier Bay, Louisiana, capturing the impacts of five major hurricanes: Barry (Category 1, 2019); the sequential storms Laura (Category 4) and Delta (Category 2), which struck within six weeks of one another in 2020; Zeta (Category 3, 2020); and Ida (Category 4, 2021). Using this data set, we aim to address three overarching questions**.**

1.  How do repeated, high-intensity hurricanes affect benthic community α- and β-diversity over time, and are ecological responses consistent across successive events?

2.  To what extent are α- and β-diversity shaped by pulse disturbances such as hurricanes relative to ongoing environmental changes, including seasonal variation, stochastic fluctuations, and longer-term “press” effects?

3.  Do α- and β-diversity exhibit similar or divergent responses across hurricane disturbances, and what do these responses reveal about the roles of niche filtering, species interactions, and diversity-dependent feedbacks during community reorganization?

By addressing these questions, we seek to disentangle the processes driving community reorganization in a system experiencing both recurrent extreme events and sustained environmental pressures.

## Methods

![**Figure 1:** Map of Terrebonne–Timbalier Bay showing benthic sampling locations (TB2B), LUMCON monitoring stations (MC: Marine Center, TB: Terrebonne Bay), and tracks of major hurricanes impacting the region during the study period (2019–2021).](images/Rplot-02.jpeg)

### Sampling Location and Changing Environment

Benthic sampling occurred in Terrebonne Bay, located along the Louisiana Gulf Coast, a broad (1,761 km2) and shallow estuarine bay (average depth 2m, Fig. 1). Terrebonne Bay is separated from the Gulf of Mexico by two barrier island chains, Timbalier Islands and Isles Dernieres and is connected by four tidal passes [@inoue2000]. The bay-estuary complex is part of an abandoned distributary of the Mississippi River. The estuary therefore no longer receives any significant riverine inputs, and the majority of its freshwater inputs are from the local drainage basin [@bianchi2009]. Tides in Terrebonne Bay are diurnal with a semi-diurnal component, ranging 20-80 cm [@inoue2000]. Circulation in the bay is impacted by winds due to shallow depths (\<3m), but strong tidal currents can form where the depth increases near tidal inlets [@inoue2000]. During the winter months, large amounts of sediment accumulation may occur just prior to a cold front passage when the wind blows from the south [@reed1989]. Since Terrebonne Bay has very limited river flow, nutrient limitation is likely to be controlled by the connection between the fringing marsh and sediments within the Bay’s water column. Terrebonne Bay is usually net heterotrophic as a result of low light availability (1% light level penetrates 30% or less of the total water column depth) and high DOC inputs from the surrounding marshes, yet can still maintain daily rates of gross primary production as high as 3-4 g C m-2 d-1 during spring and summer periods (Roberts, unpublished data). This high rate of primary production fuels high secondary production rates and ultimately supports productive fisheries in the bay [@lindstedt2005].

Terrebonne Bay has undergone dramatic landscape change over the past century, driven by a combination of rising relative sea levels, high rates of subsidence, reduced sediment delivery, and intensified storm impacts [@zou2015; @couvillion2017; @blum2023]. Relative sea‑level rise and subsidence in the basin rank among the highest in Louisiana [@jankowski2017], contributing to persistent marsh flooding and rapid loss of wetlands that historically buffered estuarine environments from open‑ocean conditions. In the lower basin, sediment deficits and saltwater intrusion through navigation canals have accelerated erosion and habitat conversion [@day2019], leading to inland migration of marine processes and the transformation of brackish marshes into open water systems. The barrier island systems that once shielded Terrebonne Bay from Gulf wave energy have concurrently diminished due to erosion, storm overwash, and sediment starvation, with some islands experiencing the highest erosion rates and overall loss of barrier land area in the region [@dingler1990]. As these islands shrink and fragment, the protective buffering of interior wetlands and estuarine habitats declines, increasing exposure to storm surge, salinity intrusion, and high‑energy conditions that facilitate further habitat degradation. These historical geomorphic and hydrologic transformations continue to occur and likely exacerbated by ongoing climate change, coastal land loss, and anthropogenic modification.

### Hurricane Events

Our sampling incorporated the impacts of four major hurricane events—Barry (Category 1, 2019), Laura/Delta (Category 4/2, 2020), Zeta (Category 3, 2020), and Ida (Category 4, 2021)—all of which directly affected Terrebonne Bay.

**Hurricane Barry** (July 13, 2019) made landfall between 7 a.m. and 1 p.m. CDT on July 13 near Vermilion Bay as a Category 1 hurricane, with sustained winds around 120.3 km h^-1^ [@brown2019; @li2023]. Although not a direct hit on our sampling site, Terrebonne Bay did experience the “dirty side” or right-front quadrant of the hurricane. The top two surges occurred in Louisiana at Caillou Lake (Dulac, LA) and Caillou Bay (Cocodrie, LA) in Terrebonne Parish near our sampling site, with surges of 2.78 m and 1.99 m, respectively [@brown2019]. The water volume transported during Hurricane Barry was 4 times that of a standard cold front [@huang2020]. Personal observation at the Louisiana Universities Marine Consortium (LUMCON) marine center in Cocodrie, LA post-event confirmed both the height of the storm surges and a considerable amount (\~5 cm) of sediment transport.

**Hurricane Laura** (August 27, 2020) made landfall near Cameron, LA, as a Category 4 system, delivering winds 246.23 km h^-1^ although winds further to the east where the sampling site is located were considerably slower [@nws2020laura]. Similar to Hurricane Barry, our sampling site occurred in the right-front quadrant of the hurricanes. Water levels during Laura increased by 4.29 to 1.64 m around the sample site [@nws2020delta]. LUMCON monitoring station observations in Terrebonne Bay recorded 74 mph sustained winds with gusts up to 107 mph during Laura. Laura traversed the region over a 24–36‐hour period, generating intense wind-driven waves and storm surge. In early October, **Hurricane Delta** (October 4, 2020) made landfall just 20km to the east of Laura as a Category 2 with highest wind speeds 160 km h^-1^ [@nws2020delta]. Although substantially weaker, Delta’s passage reinforced elevated water levels and wave energy at the site, effectively compounding the short-term environmental disturbance initiated by Laura.

**Hurricane Zeta** (October 28, 2020) Hurricane Zeta made landfall in southeastern Louisiana as a Category 3 hurricane, with peak sustained winds near 213 km h^-1^ [@blake2020; @nws2020zeta]. The storm passed directly over the sampling site near Cocodrie, LA, where it made landfall, bringing heavy rainfall, strong winds, and a significant storm surge estimated at 0.61 to 0.91m in the Terrebonne Bay area [@blake2020; @nws2020zeta].

**Hurricane Ida** (August 26, 2021) made landfall near Port Fourchon, LA, as a powerful Category 4 hurricane with peak sustained winds estimated at 240 km/h (150 mph), one of the strongest on record to impact the region [@beven2022]. Ida’s eyewall remained over Terrebonne Bay for approximately 6 to 8 hours, delivering prolonged extreme wind speeds and causing record-breaking storm surge levels that overtopped levees along the Larose–Golden Meadow line [@beven2022]. The storm generated intense rainfall, with some locations recording over 30 cm in less than 24 hours, exacerbating flooding and sediment displacement [@beven2022].

### Sampling Methods

We conducted approximately bi-monthly sampling (2018-2022) of soft-bottom, macrofaunal communities at a site in the middle of Terrebonne Bay (**Fig. 1**). Additional sampling efforts were made pre- and post- hurricane events, when possible, to increase resolution and analyze disturbance effects. During this sampling interval `r length(unique(core$Sample.Event[core$Collection.Date2 < as.POSIXct("2022-12-31")]))` sampling events occurred resulting in `r length(unique(core$Sample.ID    [core$Collection.Date2 < as.POSIXct("2022-12-31")]))` macrofaunal cores. Cores were subsampled from a Wildco 1725-F50 ponar grab (9’’ x 9’’) off a small boat. A total of 2-6 cores (7-cm diameter) were taken from each ponar grab. Total number of cores per grab depended on sediment quality, i.e. total amount of undisturbed sediment taken. Based off of species accumulation curves and prior power analysis, 12+ sub-cores were taken per sampling event to accurately characterize their diversity. The overlying water from the core top along with the top 5 cm of the core were sieved on a 300 μm mesh and preserved in 80% ETOH for later identification of species. Macrofaunal species identification was conducted by trained ecologists with consultation of taxonomic experts.

### Environmental Data

Environmental data from two LUMCON monitoring stations—Terrebonne Bay (Lat: 29.187°N, 90.6093°W) and Marine Center (Lat: 29.25333°N, 90.66333°W)—spanning 2018–2022 were imported and preprocessed using R version 4.5.1 (2025-06-13). We extracted four environmental variables for each sampling location: temperature (°C), salinity (ppt), dissolved oxygen (mg/L), and chlorophyll concentration (µg/L). These variables were included as predictors in all subsequent analyses. After loading the data sets, data type conversions were applied to ensure numerical consistency. The Terrebonne Bay monitoring station is closest to our sampling location but has data collection gaps due to damage from tropical storms, hurricanes, and ship strikes. To estimate these missing values, linear regression models were developed using corresponding Marine Center measurements as predictors for temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, and chlorophyll concentrations. Model-predicted values were imputed into the Terrebonne Bay macrofaunal sampling data set. Summary statistics (mean, minimum, maximum, and count) for each parameter were then calculated for a focal time window (1 month before a sampling event) for each sampling time.

### Analyses

Data was processed and analyzed in R version 4.5.1 (2025-06-13). Samples with no observed organisms or with low core quality were excluded. Juvenile counts were combined with adult totals for each species. Collection dates were standardized and used to classify samples by months since major hurricanes (Barry, Laura, Zeta, Ida). All data and R code to generate the analyses and manuscript are available at <https://github.com/crmcclain/terrebonne_benthic>

#### Abundance and α-Diversity

We calculated per-sample diversity using Hill numbers (q = 0, 1, 2). Hill numbers provide a unified framework for quantifying biodiversity while incorporating species abundances. As the order of 𝑞 increases, greater weight is given to the relative abundances of common species. Specifically, q=0 corresponds to species richness, q=1 to the exponential of Shannon entropy, and q=2 to the inverse Simpson index. Hill numbers were computed with the *hill_taxa()* function in the `hillR` R package [@hillR].

To examine the relationships between environmental variables and benthic community metrics, we fitted generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) using the *gamm*() function from the `mgcv` R package [@mgcv]. GAMMs were used to flexibly model nonlinear relationships between community metrics and environmental variables while accounting for repeated measures across sampling events, allowing us to isolate the effects of hurricanes on abundance and diversity. Response variables included total abundance and Hill numbers calculated per sample. Predictor variables included mean temperature (°C), salinity (ppt), dissolved oxygen (mg L⁻¹), chlorophyll-a (µg L⁻¹), and time since the most recent hurricane. Nonlinear effects of continuous predictors were modeled using smooth terms, while the random effect of sampling event was included to account for the non-independence of multiple cores collected on the same date at a given site**.** Models were estimated using restricted maximum likelihood (REML), and convergence was facilitated with increased iteration limits (maxIter = 100, msMaxIter = 100) and the nlminb optimizer. To examine temporal patterns in benthic abundance and diversity independent of measured environmental drivers, we calculated residuals from the fitted GAMMs for total abundance and Hill numbers. Residuals represent the variation in each response variable that remains after accounting for nonlinear effects of temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, chlorophyll-a, and sampling event as a random effect.

#### β-Diversity

To quantify changes in benthic community composition between consecutive sampling events, we calculated Temporal β-diversity Index (TBI) [@legendre2019]. TBI measures the dissimilarity between communities at two time points for the same site or sampling unit. It provides insight into the degree to which species abundances or presences have changed over time, partitioning changes into species Losses (B) and Gains (C). We used the percentage difference inde**x** (quantitative analog of the Sorensen index) to compute TBI for each pair of consecutive sample events, with abundances averaged per sample event. All calculations were performed using the *TBI()* function from the `adespatial` R package [@adespatial].

Because β-diversity metrics were derived from mean of pooled core-level abundances within a sampling event, repeated-measures random effects could not be incorporated. These pooled values were needed for the calculations of the β-diversity metrics. Therefore, we modeled these metrics using generalized additive models (GAMs) with the *gam()* function from package `mgcv` [@mgcv]. Predictor variables included the same environmental and hurricane covariates as in the alpha diversity models. Smooth terms captured nonlinear effects of continuous predictors, and models were fitted using REML.

#### Comparison of α- and β-Diversity

To directly compare hurricane and environmental driven responses of α- and β-diversity, we refitted alpha diversity metrics using GAMs (without random effects, because data were pooled for this) to match the modeling approach applied to beta-diversity. Standardized effect sizes were then extracted for each metric to assess the magnitude and significance of hurricane impacts. This approach allowed us to determine whether short-term, post-hurricane changes were stronger in alpha-diversity or in beta-diversity and to explore the relative contributions of environmental variables versus hurricane timing to observed patterns in benthic community composition.

To visualize the multivariate relationships among benthic community metrics, environmental variables, and hurricane timing, we conducted a multidimensional scaling (MDS) analysis. We standardized all response variables, including total abundance, Hill numbers, beta-diversity metrics (TBI, Losses, Gains), environmental variables (temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, chlorophyll-a), and hurricane timing. Hurricane timing was encoded numerically to represent sequential post-disturbance periods. We then computed a Pearson correlation matrix among all standardized variables to quantify pairwise associations. To use the correlations as distances for ordination, we converted the correlation matrix to a dissimilarity matrix. MDS was applied to this dissimilarity matrix using the *cmdscale()* function in R, producing a two-dimensional ordination that preserves the relative distances among variables. The resulting MDS axes were interpreted as relative gradients of correlated responses. Variables that clustered together or aligned along the same axis were positively correlated, whereas variables on opposite ends of an axis were negatively correlated. We note that some response variables, such as Hill numbers of different orders, are inherently correlated; q=1 and q=2 Hill numbers are derived from the same species abundance distributions and thus tend to covary. These correlations are expected and were retained to preserve the ecological gradients represented by each metric.

## Results

### Abundance and α-Diversity

In total, our benthic community data set consisted of `r ncol(core_spec_new) - 1` species and a total of `r prettyNum(sum(core_spec_new[, -1], na.rm = TRUE), big.mark = ",", scientific = FALSE)` individuals across all sampling events. Abundance and individual-based Hill numbers exhibited substantial temporal variability (**Fig. 2**). Total abundance and species richness (Hill number q = 0) showed a general increasing trend over the study period, whereas diversity measures that incorporate evenness (Hill numbers q=1 and q = 2) fluctuated considerably around a relatively stable central value. All metrics declined following hurricane events but generally recovered to pre-disturbance levels. In some cases, post-hurricane rebounds exceeded previous values, e.g., Hill numbers accounting for evenness (q = 1, 2) increased sharply following Hurricane Barry.

```{r}
#| label: First Plotting and Summary Stats
#| include: false

#### 4. Define Hurricane Dates & Shaded Year Regions ####

  hurricanes <- tibble(
    date = as.POSIXct(c("2019-07-12", "2020-08-27", "2020-10-28", "2021-08-29")),
    name = c("Barry (Cat. 1)", "Laura (Cat. 4)", "Zeta (Cat. 3)", "Ida (Cat. 4)")
  )
  
  shaded_years <- tibble(
    xmin = as.POSIXct(paste0(2018:2022, "-01-01")),
    xmax = as.POSIXct(paste0(2019:2023, "-01-01")),
    ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
    Year = factor(2018:2022),
    fill = c("#d9e6f2", "#a6bddb", "#74a9cf", "#2b8cbe", "#08589e")
  )

#### 5. Summarize Core Data ####

  # Calculate abundance, richness, Shannon diversity, and evenness
  core_summary <- core %>%
    select(-Latitude_DD, -Longitude_DD, -Latitude_DMS, -Longitude_DMS, -Station, -Depth_m) %>%
    mutate(
      Abundance = log10(rowSums(core_spec_new[-1], na.rm = TRUE) + 1),
      Richness = specnumber(core_spec_new[-1]),
      Shannon = diversity(core_spec_new[-1], index = "shannon"),
      Evenness = if_else(is.na(Shannon / log(Richness)), 0, Shannon / log(Richness)),
      Hill_q0 = hill_taxa(core_spec_new[, -1], q = 0),
      Hill_q1 = hill_taxa(core_spec_new[, -1], q = 1),
      Hill_q2 = hill_taxa(core_spec_new[, -1], q = 2)
    )

#### 6. Define Custom Plot Theme ####

  theme_craig <- function(show_legend = TRUE) {
    base <- theme_bw(base_size = 12, base_family = "Helvetica") %+replace%
      theme(
        axis.line = element_line(color = "darkgrey"),
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        strip.background = element_blank(),
        axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.title = element_text(lineheight = .8, face = "bold", hjust = 0)
      )
    if (show_legend) base + theme(legend.position = "right") else base + theme(legend.position = "none")
  }

#### 7. Helper for Plotting ####

    # Common rectangular year shading and hurricane lines for plots
    common_plot_layers <- list(
      geom_rect(data = shaded_years, aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = ymin, ymax = ymax, fill = Year),
                alpha = 0.2, inherit.aes = FALSE),
      scale_fill_manual(values = setNames(shaded_years$fill, shaded_years$Year)),
      scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "3 months", date_labels = "%b", expand = c(0, 0)),
      coord_cartesian(xlim = as.POSIXct(c("2018-06-01", "2022-12-31"))),
      geom_vline(data = hurricanes, aes(xintercept = as.numeric(date)),
                     hjust = 0.5, vjust = 0.5, color = "grey40", size = 1, linetype = "longdash"),
      geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "grey20", span = 0.2, aes(group = 1)),
      theme_craig(FALSE) + theme(legend.position = "none")
    )
    
    # Plotting function
        make_plot <- function(data, yvar, ylab_text, alpha_val = 0.8, panel_label = NULL) {
      ggplot(data, aes(x = Collection.Date2, y = .data[[yvar]])) +
        geom_point(shape = 16, size = 2, alpha = alpha_val, color = "darkgrey") +
        labs(x = "Month", y = ylab_text) +
        common_plot_layers +
        ggtitle(panel_label) +   # Add the sequential letter as title
        theme_craig() +
        theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
    }

#### 8. Generate Basic Plots ####

        
abundance_plot <- make_plot(core_summary, "Abundance", "Log10 Abundance", 0.6, "A")+
          geom_text_repel(data=hurricanes, aes(x=date, y=Inf, label=name),
                      force_pull   = 0, # do not pull toward data points
    nudge_y      = 0.05,
    direction    = "x",
    angle        = 90,
    hjust        = 0,
    segment.size = 0.2,
    max.iter = 1e4, max.time = 1,
    size=3)
        
        
hill_q0_plot   <- make_plot(core_summary, "Hill_q0", "Richness (q=0)", 0.6, "B")
hill_q1_plot   <- make_plot(core_summary, "Hill_q1", "Hill-Shannon (q=1)", 0.6, "C")
hill_q2_plot   <- make_plot(core_summary, "Hill_q2", "Hill-Simpson (q=2)", 0.6, "D")


    # Arrange summary plots in 4x1 grid with common legend
    
    summary_plots_hill <- ggarrange(
      abundance_plot,
      hill_q0_plot,
      hill_q1_plot,
      hill_q2_plot,
      ncol = 1,
      common.legend = TRUE,
      legend = "right"
    )


```

```{r}
#| label: Figure 2 Overall Plots
#| echo:  FALSE
#| message: FALSE
#| fig.cap: "<strong> Figure 2:</strong> Temporal trajectories of per-core (points) (A) abundance and Hill numbers (q = 0, 1, 2; B-D) across all sampling events. Vertical lines indicate the timing of individual hurricanes, while shaded color blocks denote calendar years. A LOESS smooth (black line, span = 0.2) is included to highlight overall temporal trends while preserving short-term fluctuations in the data."
#| fig.align: center
#| fig.width: 7
#| fig.height: 10

# Combine all plots in a row
summary_plots_hill

```

GAMM results indicated that different environmental variables influenced abundance and α-diversity metrics in distinct ways (**Table 1**). Total abundance was marginally positively associated with salinity, explaining 1% of the deviance. Species richness (Hill number q = 1) was significantly related to both dissolved oxygen and abundance, which explained 2.6% and 57.5% of deviance, respectively. Hill numbers incorporating evenness (q = 1, 2) were primarily driven by abundance, but also significantly related to chlorophyll, although it explained only \~3% of variation. Overall, and unsurprisingly, abundance emerged as a consistent and strongest predictor across all diversity measures. Environmental factors such as dissolved oxygen had significant or marginal effects on lower-order Hill numbers, whereas chlorophyll-a influenced higher-order Hill numbers more selectively.

```{r}
#| label: Environmental GAMMS for Alpha
#| include: false

 # 9.1 Recode and factor hurricane timing and season
      core_summary2<- core_summary %>%
        mutate(
          Months_Since_Hurricane = case_when(
            Months_Since_Hurricane == "Pre" ~ "Pre",
            Months_Since_Hurricane %in% c("1", "2", "3") ~ "1-3",
            Months_Since_Hurricane %in% c("4", "5") ~ "4-5"
          ),
          Months_Since_Hurricane = factor(Months_Since_Hurricane, levels = c("Pre", "1-3", "4-5")),
          Season = factor(Season, levels = c("Spring", "Summer", "Fall", "Winter"))
        )
      
  # 9.2 GAMM models 
      

    # Fit Abundance GAMM
    gamm_abun <- gamm(
      formula = Abundance ~ s(mean_temp_F) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) + 
      s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) +  Months_Since_Hurricane,
      random = list(Sample.Event = ~1),
      data = core_summary2, method = "REML",
      control = lmeControl(opt = "nlminb", maxIter = 100, msMaxIter = 100))
    
     # Fit Hill q0 GAMM
      gamm_q0 <- gamm(
      formula = Hill_q0  ~ s(mean_temp_F) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) + s(Abundance) +
      s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) +  Months_Since_Hurricane,
      random = list(Sample.Event = ~1),
      data = core_summary2, method = "REML",
      control = lmeControl(opt = "nlminb", maxIter = 100, msMaxIter = 100))
      
    
     # Fit Hill q1 GAMM
    gamm_q1 <- gamm(
      formula = Hill_q1  ~ s(mean_temp_F) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) + s(Abundance) +
      s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) +  Months_Since_Hurricane,
      random = list(Sample.Event = ~1),
      data = core_summary2, method = "REML",
      control = lmeControl(opt = "nlminb", maxIter = 100, msMaxIter = 100))
      
    
     # Fit Hill q2 GAMM
    gamm_q2 <- gamm(
      formula = Hill_q2 ~ s(mean_temp_F) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) + s(Abundance) +
      s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) +  Months_Since_Hurricane,
      random = list(Sample.Event = ~1),
      data = core_summary2, method = "REML",
      control = lmeControl(opt = "nlminb", maxIter = 100, msMaxIter = 100))

    
   # 9.3 Function to summarize GAMM models
summarize_gamm_model <- function(model, model_name) {
  sm <- summary(model$gam)
  
  reml_score <- if (is.numeric(model$gam$gcv.ubre)) {
    round(model$gam$gcv.ubre, 3)
  } else {
    NA
  }
  
  data.frame(
    Model = model_name,
    Term = rownames(sm$s.table),
    EDF = round(sm$s.table[, "edf"], 2),
    `F-stat` = round(sm$s.table[, "F"], 2),
    `p-value` = signif(sm$s.table[, "p-value"], 3),
    check.names = FALSE
  )
}

#9.3.5 Function to compute approximate partial R² for each smooth term in a GAM
      partial_r2_gam <- function(gam_model) {
        # Get summary of the GAM
        sm <- summary(gam_model)
        
        # Extract smooth term names
        terms <- rownames(sm$s.table)
        
        # Residual degrees of freedom
        df_resid <- sm$residual.df
        
        # Approximate partial R² for each smooth: F * EDF / (F * EDF + residual df)
        partial_r2 <- (sm$s.table[, "F"] * sm$s.table[, "edf"]) / 
                      (sm$s.table[, "F"] * sm$s.table[, "edf"] + df_resid)
        
        # Return as a named vector
        names(partial_r2) <- terms
        return(round(partial_r2, 3))
      }
    
      
     # 9.4 Generate summaries and combine into one table
      abun_summary  <- summarize_gamm_model(gamm_abun,  "Abundance")
      hill0_summary    <- summarize_gamm_model(gamm_q0,  "Richness (q=0)")
      hill1_summary   <- summarize_gamm_model(gamm_q1,  "Hill-Shannon (q=1)")
      hill2_summary    <- summarize_gamm_model(gamm_q2,  "Hill-Simpson (q=2)")
      
     # Compute partial R² separately
        abun_summary$Partial_R2  <- partial_r2_gam(gamm_abun$gam)[abun_summary$Term]
        hill0_summary$Partial_R2 <- partial_r2_gam(gamm_q0$gam)[hill0_summary$Term]
        hill1_summary$Partial_R2 <- partial_r2_gam(gamm_q1$gam)[hill1_summary$Term]
        hill2_summary$Partial_R2 <- partial_r2_gam(gamm_q2$gam)[hill2_summary$Term]

# Combine into one table
      model_stats_table <- dplyr::bind_rows(abun_summary, hill0_summary, hill1_summary,
                                            hill2_summary) %>%
        dplyr::filter(`p-value` <= 0.10)%>%
        rename(`Partial R.sq` = Partial_R2)

```

```{r}
#| label: Table 1 GAMMS
#| echo: false
      
    # 9.5 Pretty Kable Table
      
 model_stats_table %>%
  mutate(Term = case_when(
    Term == "s(mean_salinity_ppt)" ~ "Salinity",
    Term == "s(mean_DO_mg.L)" ~ "Dissolved oxygen",
    Term == "s(Abundance)" ~ "Abundance",
    Term == "s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L)" ~ "Chlorophyll",
    TRUE ~ Term  # keep any other terms unchanged
  )) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    row.names = FALSE,
    caption = "<strong>Table 1:</strong> Summary of generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) for abundance and Hill numbers (q = 0, 1, 2). Only terms with p ≤ 0.10 are shown. The table includes estimated effective degrees of freedom (EDF), F-statistics,snd p-values. Models account for the random effect of sampling event. Residuals degrees of freedom for abundance and Hill numbers (q = 0, 1, 2) are 394.4, 389.7, 386.3, and 387.1 respectively.",
    digits = 3,
    format = "pandoc",  # ensures Word compatibility
    booktabs = TRUE
  )
 
```

Visual inspection of the raw, uncorrected (i.e., before accounting for environmental variables) data revealed clear declines in abundance and Hill numbers following hurricanes, with some evidence of partial recovery approximately 4–5 months post-disturbance (**Fig. 3**). After accounting for environmental effects, residual impacts of hurricanes on benthic communities were evident in both abundance and α-diversity metrics (**Table 2)**. Total abundance decreased significantly in the first 1–3 months post-hurricane and remained lower in months 4–5. Richness (q = 0) showed strong declines during months 1–3 and months 4–5, indicating substantial short-term losses in the number of species. Higher-order Hill numbers also declined significantly following hurricanes. Hill number q = 1 decreased in both months 1–3 and 4–5 post-disturbance, while Hill number q = 2 showed significant declines across both time intervals, although effect sizes were smaller than for richness. Overall, these results suggest that hurricanes produced immediate reductions in abundance and richness, with the strongest effects occurring within the first three months, while impacts on evenness occurring over longer time frames.

```{r}
#| label: Hurricane Table Calculations
#| include: false

# 10.1 Function to extract parametric coefficients from a gamm object
extract_param_coeff <- function(model, model_name) {
  tidy_df <- broom::tidy(model$gam, parametric = TRUE) %>%
    mutate(Model = model_name) %>%
    select(Model, term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value)
  
  return(tidy_df)
}

# 10.2 Apply to models
param_abun <- extract_param_coeff(gamm_abun, "Abundance")
param_q0   <- extract_param_coeff(gamm_q0, "Richness (q=0)")
param_q1   <- extract_param_coeff(gamm_q1, "Hill-Shannon (q=1)")
param_q2   <- extract_param_coeff(gamm_q2, "Hill-Simpson (q=2)")

# 10.3  Combine into a single table
param_table <- bind_rows(param_abun, param_q0, param_q1, param_q2)

# 10.4 Clean param_table
param_table_clean <- param_table %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%  # drop intercept
  mutate(
    Term = recode(term,
                  "Months_Since_Hurricane1-3" = "Months 1-3",
                  "Months_Since_Hurricane4-5" = "Months 4-5"),
    Model = Model,
    Estimate = estimate,
    `Std. error` = std.error,
    `T-value` = statistic,
    `P-value` = p.value
  ) %>%
  select(Model, Term, Estimate, `Std. error`, `T-value`, `P-value`)

```

```{r}
#| label: Hurricane Table 2
#| echo: false

# 10.5 Create kable with formatting
param_table_clean %>%
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    col.names = c("Model", "Term", "Estimate", "Std. error", "T-value", "P-value"),
    caption = "Table 2: Parametric coefficients from GAMMs for Months Since Hurricane for abundance and Hill metrics (q = 0, 1, 2). Intercept not shown.  Residuals degrees of freedom for abundance and Hill numbers (q = 0, 1, 2) are 394.4, 389.7, 386.3, and 387.1 respectively. Each factor level term has 1 df.",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    format = "pandoc"
  )



```

```{r}
#| label: Figure 3 Hurricane Effects
#| echo:  FALSE
#| message: FALSE
#| fig.cap: "<strong> Figure 3: </strong>Temporal distributions of per-core (A) abundance and Hill numbers (q = 0, 1, 2; B-D) across months since hurricanes. Boxplots show the range, interquartile spread, and outliers for each metric. These values are raw, uncorrected for environmental covariates, so they reflect the total observed variation in each metric following hurricanes."
#| fig.align: center
#| fig.width: 8
#| fig.height: 4


# 10.6 Gather the data into long format
core_long <- core_summary2 %>%
  select(Months_Since_Hurricane, Abundance, Hill_q0, Hill_q1, Hill_q2) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Abundance, Hill_q0, Hill_q1, Hill_q2),
    names_to = "Metric",
    values_to = "Value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Metric = recode(Metric,
                    Abundance = "Log10 Abundance",
                    Hill_q0 = "Richness (q=0)",
                    Hill_q1 = "Hill-Shannon (q=1)",
                    Hill_q2 = "Hill-Simpson (q=2)"),
    Metric = factor(Metric, levels = c("Log10 Abundance", "Richness (q=0)", "Hill-Shannon (q=1)", "Hill-Simpson (q=2)"))
  )



# Create a small dataframe with letters for each facet
facet_letters <- core_long %>%
  distinct(Metric) %>%           # one row per metric
  arrange(Metric) %>%
  mutate(
    Letter = c("A", "B", "C", "D"),   # one letter per metric
    x = 0.5,                          # middle of x axis
    y = core_long %>%
          group_by(Metric) %>%
          summarise(max_val = max(Value, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
          pull(max_val) * 1.05        # 5% above max for that metric
  )


# 10.7 Create the boxplots
ggplot(core_long, aes(x = factor(Months_Since_Hurricane), y = Value)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue3", outlier.color = "grey30") +
  facet_wrap(~Metric, scales = "free_y") +  # free_y to allow different y-axis scales
  labs(
    x = "Months Since Hurricane",
    y = "Value",
  ) +
  theme_craig() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "grey20", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
  )+
   geom_text(data = facet_letters,
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = Letter),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    fontface = "bold",
    hjust = 0
  ) 

```

### β-Diversity

Visually, temporal β-diversity (TBI) revealed pulses of community turnover following major hurricane disturbances (**Fig. 4**). The largest increase in TBI occurred after Hurricane Barry (2019), indicating the strongest single-event disruption to benthic community structure across the time series. Additional but smaller TBI increases followed Hurricanes Zeta (2020) and Ida (2021). After Ida, TBI initially declined toward December 2021 but then rose sharply again the following spring, suggesting a delayed secondary reorganization of the community. Patterns in the “Proportional Loss” component showed four relatively similar peaks in the time series corresponding directly after Hurricanes Barry and Ida and then again 6-7 months post both hurricanes. Losses increased moderately after the Laura and Zeta period but were relatively small compared to Barry and Ida. Following Ida, losses surged sharply, then dropped quickly through the winter, before rising again to another major peak in spring 2022. “Proportional Gains” showed a complementary but distinct pattern. Gains increased steadily from September through December 2019 after Barry, indicating species additions or abundances increases following the disturbance. Modest gain-driven turnover occurred from January–March after the Laura and Zeta hurricanes. A substantial pulse of gains emerged again from December 2021 through March 2022 following Ida, demonstrating a strong influx or proliferation of taxa. Together, these results indicate that hurricanes consistently correlated with elevated temporal turnover, but the mechanisms differed among events. Barry was strongly loss-driven, Ida showed a mix of immediate loss and delayed gain-driven turnover, and several large transitions, particularly in summer 2020 and spring 2022, occurred well delayed of immediate storm impacts.

```{r}
#| label: Temporal Beta Diversity Set Up
#| include: false


#11.1 Convert to triplet (long) format
  core_spec_long <- core_spec_new %>%
    pivot_longer(
      cols = -Sample.ID,           # all columns except Sample.ID
      names_to = "Species",        # new column for species
      values_to = "Abundance"      # new column for abundance
    ) %>%
    filter(Abundance > 0)      %>%  # remove rows with zero abundance
    separate(
      Sample.ID,
      into = c("Site", "Sample.Event", "CoreNumber"),  # split into three parts
      sep = "-",                                       # delimiter is "-"
      remove = FALSE                                  # keep original Sample.ID
    )   

#11.2 Summarize abundance per sample event
  sample_events_spec <- core_spec_long %>%
    group_by(Species,Sample.Event) %>%   
    summarise(
      Ab_mean = round(mean(Abundance, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      Ab_sd   = round(sd(Abundance, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      Ab_se   = round(sd(Abundance, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()), 2),
      Ab_n_species      = n(),   # number of cores with non-zero abundance
      .groups = "drop"
    )

#11.3  Convert back to wide format
  sample_events_wide <- sample_events_spec %>%
    select(Sample.Event, Species, Ab_mean) %>%  # keep only what we need
    pivot_wider(
      names_from = Species,     # species become columns
      values_from = Ab_mean,    # cell values are mean abundance
      values_fill = 0           # fill missing combinations with 0
    )

#11.4 Sample Event Summary
  sample_event_summary <- core_summary2 %>%
  group_by(Sample.Event) %>%
  summarise(
    # Contextual / environmental data (take first since constant within event)
    Year = first(Year),
    Month = first(Month),
    Season = first(Season),
    Months_Since_Hurricane = first(Months_Since_Hurricane),
    mean_temp_C = first(mean_temp_C),
    mean_salinity_ppt = first(mean_salinity_ppt),
    mean_DO_mg.L = first(mean_DO_mg.L),
    mean_chlorophyll_ug.L = first(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L),
    Date = first(Collection.Date),
    
    # Community metrics across cores
    n_cores = n(),
    Abundance_mean = mean(Abundance, na.rm = TRUE),
    Abundance_sd   = sd(Abundance, na.rm = TRUE),
    Hill_q0_mean   = mean(Hill_q0, na.rm = TRUE),
    Hill_q0_sd     = sd(Hill_q0, na.rm = TRUE),
    Hill_q1_mean   = mean(Hill_q1, na.rm = TRUE),
    Hill_q1_sd     = sd(Hill_q1, na.rm = TRUE),
    Hill_q2_mean   = mean(Hill_q2, na.rm = TRUE),
    Hill_q2_sd     = sd(Hill_q2, na.rm = TRUE),
    Richness_mean  = mean(Richness, na.rm = TRUE),
    Shannon_mean   = mean(Shannon, na.rm = TRUE),
    Evenness_mean  = mean(Evenness, na.rm = TRUE),
    
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    # Standard errors
    Abundance_se = Abundance_sd / sqrt(n_cores),
    Hill_q0_se   = Hill_q0_sd / sqrt(n_cores),
    Hill_q1_se   = Hill_q1_sd / sqrt(n_cores),
    Hill_q2_se   = Hill_q2_sd / sqrt(n_cores)
  )

  sample_event_summary <- sample_event_summary %>%
  mutate(Date = as.POSIXct(Date))

#11.5 Create numeric ordering from Sample.Event (e.g., "18.6" → Year = 2018, Month = 6)
sample_events_wide <- sample_events_wide %>%
  mutate(
    Year = 2000 + as.integer(str_extract(Sample.Event, "^[0-9]{2}")),  # assume 2000+year
    Month = as.integer(str_extract(Sample.Event, "(?<=\\.)[0-9]{1,2}")),
    Order = Year + Month/12
  ) %>%
  arrange(Order)

#11.6 Identify species columns
species_cols <- setdiff(names(sample_events_wide), c("Sample.Event", "Year", "Month", "Order"))
```

```{r}
#| label: Temporal Beta Diversity Calculations
#| include: false


#11.7 # Initialize vectors to store outputs
TBI_values <- rep(NA, nrow(sample_events_wide))
B_prop_values  <- rep(NA, nrow(sample_events_wide))
C_prop_values  <- rep(NA, nrow(sample_events_wide))
D_values      <- rep(NA, nrow(sample_events_wide))
TBI_change     <- rep(NA, nrow(sample_events_wide))
TBI_t_test     <- rep(NA, nrow(sample_events_wide))

#11.8 Loop through consecutive events
for(i in 2:nrow(sample_events_wide)) {
  mat1 <- as.matrix(sample_events_wide[i - 1, species_cols])
  mat2 <- as.matrix(sample_events_wide[i, species_cols])
  
  tbi_res <- TBI(mat1, mat2, method = "%difference", nperm = 0, test.BC=TRUE)
  
   # Assign results
  TBI_values[i]    <- tbi_res$TBI
  B_prop_values[i] <- tbi_res$BCD.mat[, "B/(2A+B+C)"]
  C_prop_values[i] <- tbi_res$BCD.mat[, "C/(2A+B+C)"]
  D_values[i]      <- tbi_res$BCD.mat[, "D=(B+C)/(2A+B+C)"]
  TBI_change[i]    <- tbi_res$BCD.mat[, "Change"]
  TBI_t_test[i]    <- tbi_res$t.test_B.C
  
}

#11.9 Add TBI to summary table
sample_event_summary$TBI        <- TBI_values
sample_event_summary$B_prop     <- B_prop_values
sample_event_summary$C_prop     <- C_prop_values
sample_event_summary$D          <- D_values
sample_event_summary$Change     <- TBI_change
sample_event_summary$TBI_t_test <- TBI_t_test
```

```{r}
#| label: Figure 4 Beta Div Plot
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-align: center
#| fig.cap: "<strong> Figure 4:</strong> Temporal β-diversity dynamics of the benthic community across the study period. (A) Temporal Beta-diversity Index (TBI) calculated between consecutive sampling events, representing the overall magnitude of community turnover. (B) Proportional contributions of Losses (B-components) and (C) Gains (C-components) to total compositional change. Points represent individual sampling events."
#| fig-height: 10
#| fig-width: 7


TBI_plot <- ggplot(sample_event_summary, aes(x = Date, y = TBI)) +
        geom_point(shape = 16, size = 2, alpha = .6, color = "darkgrey") +
        labs(x = "Month", y = "Temporal Beta-Diversity (TBI)", , title="A") +
        common_plot_layers +
        ggtitle("A") +
        theme_craig() +
        theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))+
      geom_text_repel(data=hurricanes, aes(x=date, y=Inf, label=name),
                      force_pull   = 0, # do not pull toward data points
                      nudge_y      = 0.05, direction    = "x", angle        = 90, 
                      hjust        = 0, segment.size = 0.2, max.iter = 1e4, max.time = 1,
                      size=3)
    
Losses_plot <- ggplot(sample_event_summary, aes(x = Date, y = B_prop)) +
        geom_point(shape = 16, size = 2, alpha = .6, color = "darkgrey") +
        labs(x = "Month", y = "Proportional Losses (B)", title="B") +
        common_plot_layers +
        ggtitle("B") +
        theme_craig() +
        theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

Gaines_plot <- ggplot(sample_event_summary, aes(x = Date, y = C_prop)) +
        geom_point(shape = 16, size = 2, alpha = .6, color = "darkgrey") +
        labs(x = "Month", y = "Proportional Gains (C)", title="C") +
        common_plot_layers +
        theme_craig() +
        theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

sample_event_summary <- sample_event_summary %>%
  mutate(
    Change_clean = str_trim(Change),   # remove leading/trailing spaces
    Change_arrow = case_when(
      Change_clean == "+" ~ 1,
      Change_clean == "-" ~ -1,
      Change_clean == "0" ~ 0,
      TRUE                ~ 0
    )
  )


ggarrange(TBI_plot, Losses_plot, Gaines_plot, ncol=1, common.legend = TRUE, legend = "right")

```

```{r}
#| label: Environmental GAMMS for Beta
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

      
  # 12.2 GAMM models 
      

    # Fit TBI
    gam_TBI <- gam(TBI ~ s(mean_temp_C) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) +
                    s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) + Months_Since_Hurricane,
                    data = sample_event_summary,
                    method = "REML")
    
     # Fit Losses
      gam_B_prop <- gam(B_prop ~ s(mean_temp_C) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) +
                    s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) + Months_Since_Hurricane,
                    data = sample_event_summary,
                    method = "REML")
      
    
     # Fit Gains
    gam_C_prop  <- gam(C_prop ~ s(mean_temp_C) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) +
                    s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) + Months_Since_Hurricane,
                    data = sample_event_summary,
                    method = "REML")
      
    #12.3 function for summarizing gam
            summarize_gam_model <- function(model, model_name) {
          if (!"gam" %in% class(model)) stop("Model must be a gam object")
          
          sm <- summary(model)
          
          data.frame(
            Model = model_name,
            Term = rownames(sm$s.table),
            EDF = round(sm$s.table[, "edf"], 2),
            `F-stat` = round(sm$s.table[, "F"], 2),
            `p-value` = signif(sm$s.table[, "p-value"], 3),
            `R.sq (explained deviance)` = round(sm$r.sq, 3),
            check.names = FALSE
          )
        }

  
   
            
    # 12.4 Generate summaries for beta-diversity GAMs
TBI_summary    <- summarize_gam_model(gam_TBI,   "TBI")
B_prop_summary <- summarize_gam_model(gam_B_prop, "Losses")
C_prop_summary <- summarize_gam_model(gam_C_prop, "Gains")

# Compute partial R² separately
TBI_summary$Partial_R2    <- partial_r2_gam(gam_TBI)[TBI_summary$Term]
B_prop_summary$Partial_R2 <- partial_r2_gam(gam_B_prop)[B_prop_summary$Term]
C_prop_summary$Partial_R2 <- partial_r2_gam(gam_C_prop)[C_prop_summary$Term]

# Combine into one table
beta_model_stats_table <- dplyr::bind_rows(TBI_summary, B_prop_summary, C_prop_summary) %>%
  dplyr::filter(`p-value` <= 0.10) %>%
  select(-`R.sq (explained deviance)`) %>%
  rename(`Partial R.sq` = Partial_R2)


```

```{r}
#| label: Table 3 GAMS for Beta
#| echo: false
      
    # 12.5 Pretty Kable Table
      
beta_model_stats_table %>%
  mutate(Term = case_when(
    Term == "s(mean_salinity_ppt)" ~ "Salinity",
    Term == "s(mean_DO_mg.L)" ~ "Dissolved oxygen",
    Term == "s(mean_temp_C)" ~ "Temperature",
    Term == "s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L)" ~ "Chlorophyll",
    TRUE ~ Term  # keep any other terms unchanged
  )) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    row.names = FALSE,
    caption = "<strong>Table 3</strong>: Summary of generalized additive mixed models (GAMs) for Temporal Beta-diversity Index (TBI), proportional contributions of Losses (B-components) and Gains (C-components). Only terms with p ≤ 0.10 are shown. The table includes estimated effective degrees of freedom (EDF), F-statistics, p-values, and the proportion of explained deviance (R²). Residual degrees of freedom for TBI, Losses, and Gaines are 14.9, 16.6, and 19.4.",
    digits = 3,
    booktabs = TRUE,
    format = "pandoc"
  )
```

GAM results indicated that different environmental variables influenced temporal β-diversity and its components in distinct ways (**Table 3)**. TBI was strongly influenced by dissolved oxygen, which explained the largest proportion of variation (partial R² = 0.53) despite its marginal significance, while temperature also contributed substantially and was statistically significant (partial R² = 0.42). “Proportional Losses” were significantly related to temperature, which explained 42% of the variation in Losses. Overall, temperature consistently predicted compositional changes, whereas dissolved oxygen, despite explaining a large fraction of variation, showed more variability across responses. In contrast, none of the measured environmental variables were significant predictors of “Proportional Gains”.

```{r}
#| label: Figure 5 Hurricane Effects
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-cap: "<strong> Figure 5:</strong> Distributions for (A) Temporal Beta-diversity Index (TBI), proportional contributions of (B) Losses (B-components) and (C) Gains (C-components) across months since hurricanes. Boxplots show the range, interquartile spread, and outliers for each metric. These values are raw, uncorrected for environmental covariates, so they reflect the total observed variation in each metric following hurricanes."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 8


# 13.1. Gather the beta-diversity metrics into long format
beta_long <- sample_event_summary %>%
  select(Months_Since_Hurricane, TBI, B_prop, C_prop) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(TBI, B_prop, C_prop),
    names_to = "Metric",
    values_to = "Value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Metric = recode(Metric,
                    TBI = "Temporal Beta-Diversity (TBI)",
                    B_prop = "Proportional Losses (B)",
                    C_prop = "Proportional Gains (C)"),
    Metric = factor(Metric, levels = c(
      "Temporal Beta-Diversity (TBI)",
      "Proportional Losses (B)",
      "Proportional Gains (C)"
    ))
  )

# Create letters for each facet
facet_letters2 <- beta_long %>%
  distinct(Metric) %>%
  arrange(Metric) %>%
  mutate(
    Letter = c("A", "B", "C"),  # adjust based on number of facets
    x = 0.5,  # middle of x-axis
    y = sapply(Metric, function(m) max(beta_long$Value[beta_long$Metric == m], na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05)  # top of each facet ignoring NAs
  )



# 13.2. Create boxplots for beta-diversity metrics
ggplot(beta_long, aes(x = factor(Months_Since_Hurricane), y = Value)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue3", outlier.color = "grey30") +
  facet_wrap(~Metric, scales = "free_y") +  # allows each metric to have its own y-axis scale
  labs(
    x = "Months Since Hurricane",
    y = "Value"
  ) +
  theme_craig() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )+
   geom_text(
    data = facet_letters2,
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = Letter),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    fontface = "bold",
    hjust = 0
  ) 

```

Visual inspection of the temporal β-diversity metrics (**Fig. 5**) revealed distinct patterns following hurricane events. TBI increased from pre-disturbance to months 1–3 and further to months 4–5, indicating progressive compositional change over time. “Proportional Losses” were slightly elevated in the first 1–3 months post-hurricane but declined sharply by months 4–5, whereas “Proportional Gains” remained relatively stable from pre-disturbance to 1–3 months but increased markedly by months 4–5. Consequently, TBI in the early post-hurricane interval (1–3 months) appears primarily driven by species losses, whereas compositional change at months 4–5 was dominated by species gains.

```{r}
#| label: Hurricane Table 4 for Beta-Diversity
#| echo: false

# 14.1 Function to extract parametric coefficients from a gam object
extract_param_coeff_gam <- function(model, model_name) {
  tidy_df <- broom::tidy(model, parametric = TRUE) %>%
    mutate(Model = model_name) %>%
    select(Model, term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value)
  return(tidy_df)
}

# 14.2 Apply to your GAMs
param_TBI <- extract_param_coeff_gam(gam_TBI, "TBI")
param_B   <- extract_param_coeff_gam(gam_B_prop, "Losses (B-Components)")
param_C   <- extract_param_coeff_gam(gam_C_prop, "Gains (C-Components)")

# 14.3 Combine into a single table
param_table_beta <- bind_rows(param_TBI, param_B, param_C)

# 14.4 Clean param_table for presentation
param_table_clean_beta <- param_table_beta %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%  # drop intercept
  mutate(
    Term = recode(term,
                  "Months_Since_Hurricane1-3" = "Months 1-3",
                  "Months_Since_Hurricane4-5" = "Months 4-5"),
    Model = Model,
    Estimate = estimate,
    `Std. error` = std.error,
    `T-value` = statistic,
    `P-value` = p.value
  ) %>%
  select(Model, Term, Estimate, `Std. error`, `T-value`, `P-value`)

# 14.5 Create kable with formatting
param_table_clean_beta %>%
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    col.names = c("Model", "Term", "Estimate", "Std. error", "T-value", "P-value"),
    caption = "<strong>Table 4</strong>: Parametric coefficients from GAMs for Months Since Hurricane for Temporal Beta-diversity metrics (TBI, Losses, Gains). Intercepts not shown. Residual degrees of freedom for TBI, Losses, and Gaines are 14.9, 16.6, and 19.4.Parametric terms have 1 df each.",
    format = "pandoc"
  )


```

After accounting for environmental effects, residual impacts of hurricanes on benthic community composition were generally absent (**Table 4**). TBI showed small increases in months 1–3 and 4–5 post-hurricane, but neither change was statistically significant. Losses were slightly elevated in months 1–3 and declined in months 4–5, without significant effects. Gains increased in months 4–5 post-hurricane, with a marginal effect (p = 0.084). Thus, once environmental effects were accounted for, there was a modest tendency for compositional gains to increase β-diversity in the months following hurricane events.

```{r}
#| label: Compare the responses of abundance, alpha diversity (Hill numbers), and beta diversity (TBI/B/C)
#| echo: false

#15.1 Standardize the response variables

sample_event_summary_std <- sample_event_summary %>%
  mutate(
    Abundance_z = scale(Abundance_mean),
    Hill_q0_z   = scale(Hill_q0_mean),
    Hill_q1_z   = scale(Hill_q1_mean),
    Hill_q2_z   = scale(Hill_q2_mean),
    TBI_z       = scale(TBI),
    B_prop_z    = scale(B_prop),
    C_prop_z    = scale(C_prop)
  )

#15.2 Fit models for each response


# Alpha diversity (Hill numbers)
gam_q0 <- gam(Hill_q0_z ~ s(mean_temp_C) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) +
              s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) + Months_Since_Hurricane,
              data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")

gam_q1 <- gam(Hill_q1_z ~ s(mean_temp_C) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) +
              s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) + Months_Since_Hurricane,
              data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")

gam_q2 <- gam(Hill_q2_z ~ s(mean_temp_C) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) +
              s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) + Months_Since_Hurricane,
              data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")

# Abundance
gam_abun <- gam(Abundance_z ~ s(mean_temp_C) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) +
                s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) + Months_Since_Hurricane,
                data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")

# Beta diversity
gam_TBI <- gam(TBI_z ~ s(mean_temp_C) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) +
               s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) + Months_Since_Hurricane,
               data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")
gam_B   <- gam(B_prop_z ~ s(mean_temp_C) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) +
               s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) + Months_Since_Hurricane,
               data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")
gam_C   <- gam(C_prop_z ~ s(mean_temp_C) + s(mean_salinity_ppt) + s(mean_DO_mg.L) +
               s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L) + Months_Since_Hurricane,
               data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")


#15.3. Extract effect sizes

extract_gam_effects <- function(gam_model, metric_name) {
  sm <- summary(gam_model)
  data.frame(
    Metric = metric_name,
    Term   = rownames(sm$s.table),
    EDF    = sm$s.table[, "edf"],
    F      = sm$s.table[, "F"],
    p      = sm$s.table[, "p-value"]
  )
}

effects_list <- list(
  extract_gam_effects(gam_abun, "Abundance"),
  extract_gam_effects(gam_q0, "Hill_q0"),
  extract_gam_effects(gam_q1, "Hill_q1"),
  extract_gam_effects(gam_q2, "Hill_q2"),
  extract_gam_effects(gam_TBI, "TBI"),
  extract_gam_effects(gam_B, "Losses"),
  extract_gam_effects(gam_C, "Gains")
)

effects_table <- do.call(rbind, effects_list)



#15.4 Function to extract hurricane effects from a GAM
extract_hurricane_effects <- function(model, model_name) {
  sm <- broom::tidy(model, parametric = TRUE)
  
  sm %>%
    filter(grepl("Months_Since_Hurricane", term)) %>%  # keep only hurricane months
    mutate(
      Model = model_name,
      EffectSize = abs(estimate / std.error)  # standardized effect size (t-value)
    ) %>%
    select(Model, term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value, EffectSize)
}

# 15.5 Apply to all relevant GAMs
hurr_abun <- extract_hurricane_effects(gam_abun, "Abundance")
hurr_q0   <- extract_hurricane_effects(gam_q0, "Richness (q=0)")
hurr_q1   <- extract_hurricane_effects(gam_q1, "Hill-Shannon (q=1)")
hurr_q2   <- extract_hurricane_effects(gam_q2, "Hill-Simpson (q=2)")
hurr_TBI  <- extract_hurricane_effects(gam_TBI, "TBI")
hurr_B    <- extract_hurricane_effects(gam_B_prop, "Losses (B-Components)")
hurr_C    <- extract_hurricane_effects(gam_C_prop, "Gains (C-Components)")

#15.6 Combine into one table
hurricane_effects <- bind_rows(hurr_abun, hurr_q0, hurr_q1, hurr_q2, hurr_TBI, hurr_B, hurr_C)

#15.7 Clean up term names for readability
hurricane_effects <- hurricane_effects %>%
  mutate(
    Term = recode(term,
                  "Months_Since_Hurricane1-3" = "Months 1-3",
                  "Months_Since_Hurricane4-5" = "Months 4-5")
  ) %>%
  arrange(Model, Term)

# 15.8 Prepare hurricane effects
hurr_plot_data <- hurricane_effects %>%
  mutate(
    TermType = "Hurricane",
    Metric = Model,
    EffectLabel = Term,
    Significance = ifelse(p.value <= 0.05, "Significant", "Not significant")
  ) %>%
  select(Metric, EffectLabel, estimate, std.error, Significance, TermType)

# 15.9. Prepare environmental effects
env_plot_data <- effects_table %>%
  mutate(
    TermType = "Environmental",
    EffectLabel = Term,
    Significance = ifelse(p <= 0.05, "Significant", "Not significant")
  ) %>%
  rename(estimate = F) %>%  # optionally use F as effect size; or use EDF
  mutate(estimate = EDF) %>% # using EDF as approximate effect size if desired
  select(Metric, EffectLabel, estimate, Significance, TermType)

# 15.10. Combine datasets
plot_data <- bind_rows(
  hurr_plot_data %>% mutate(std.error = NA), # hurricane already has estimate
  env_plot_data %>% mutate(std.error = NA)   # env effects don't need error bars
)

# 15.11 Standardize metric names
plot_data <- plot_data %>%
  filter(Metric != "Abundance") %>%
  mutate(
    Metric = recode(Metric,
                    "Hill_q0" = "Richness (q=0)",
                        "Hill Number q=0" = "Richness (q=0)",
                    "Hill_q1" = "Hill-Shannon (q=1)",
                      "Hill Number q=1" = "Hill-Shannon (q=1)",
                    "Hill_q2" = "Hill-Simpson (q=2)",
                        "Hill Number q=2" = "Hill-Simpson (q=2)",
                    "Losses (B-Components)" = "Losses",
                    "Gains (C-Components)" = "Gains"),
    Metric = factor(Metric, levels = c(
      "Richness (q=0)",
      "Hill-Shannon (q=1)",
      "Hill-Simpson (q=2)",
      "TBI",
      "Losses",
      "Gains")),
    EffectLabel = recode(EffectLabel,
                          "s(mean_chlorophyll_ug.L)" = "Chloro",
                          "s(mean_DO_mg.L)" = "DO",
                          "s(mean_salinity_ppt)" = "Salinity",
                          "s(mean_temp_C)" = "Temp",
                          .default = EffectLabel),
    EffectLabel = factor(EffectLabel, levels = c(
      "Months 1-3",
      "Months 4-5",
      "Chloro",
      "DO",
      "Salinity",
      "Temp")))

# 15.12. Make the plot
hurr2 <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = EffectLabel, y = estimate)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = TermType, shape = Significance), size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Hurricane" = "steelblue3", "Environmental" = "grey50")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("Not significant" = 15, "Significant" = 8)) +
  facet_wrap(~Metric, ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    x = "Term",
    y = "Effect size / EDF",
    color = "Term Type",
    shape = "Significance", 
    title = "B"
  ) +
 theme_craig() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "grey20", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
  )


```

### Comparison of α- and β-Diversity

Analysis of raw hurricane effects (without accounting for environmental variables) revealed that lower-order α-diversity metrics were most strongly impacted in the immediate months following disturbance (**Fig. 6A**). Hill number q = 0 declined significantly in months 1–3 and 4–5 post-hurricane. Hill number q = 1 decreased significantly in months 1–3 and showed a marginal, non-significant decline in months 4–5. Hill number q = 2 declined significantly in months 1–3 but not in months 4–5. In contrast, β-diversity metrics exhibited weaker or non-significant responses. TBI showed a small increase in months 4–5 but was not significant. Losses were slightly elevated in months 1–3 and decreased by months 4–5. Gains was the only β-diversity metric to show a significant and dramatic increase in months 4–5. Overall, α-diversity, responded most strongly to hurricane disturbance, whereas β-diversity responses were weaker with the exception of the longer term response of Gains.

After accounting for environmental changes, hurricane effects were most pronounced in the immediate months following disturbance, particularly in lower-order diversity metrics (Fig. 6B). Hill number q = 0 and q = 1 exhibited the strongest short-term responses, with decreases in the first 1–3 months post-hurricane. TBI increases slightly after hurricane events, but hurricane-driven changes were not statistically significant (**Fig. 6**). Environmental factors had more selective effects: dissolved oxygen significantly influenced Hill q = 1, while temperature marginally affected TBI and Losses, and other variables (salinity, chlorophyll-a) were generally weak predictors across metrics.

Overall, hurricanes produced the strongest immediate changes in α-diversity once accounting for environmental changes. Environmental variables contributed primarily to variation in turnover. This pattern is evident in the MDS ordination (**Fig. 6C**) visualizing the multivariate relationships among the metrics, environmental factors, and hurricane timing based on the correlation matrix of standardized response variables. α-diversity metrics (Hill numbers q = 0–2) and abundance clustered together in the negative direction along axis two, whereas hurricane timing was positive along the same axis, highlighting strong negative correlations between immediate post-hurricane periods and α-diversity. It is important to note that α-diversity metrics (Hill numbers q = 0–2) are strongly correlated with total abundance and each other, as reflected in the MDS clustering. Consequently, some of the observed short-term declines in α-diversity following hurricanes may partially reflect decreases in overall abundance, particularly for q = 1 and q = 2, which incorporate evenness. β-diversity metrics (TBI, Proportional Losses, Proportional Gains) separated along axis one in relation to environmental gradients: TBI and gains were positively associated with dissolved oxygen, chlorophyll-a, and salinity, whereas Losses were positively associated with temperature. Together, the results indicate hurricanes most strongly influence short-term α-diversity, while environmental factors contribute more selectively to turnover and community reassembly.

```{r}
#| label: Compare the responses of abundance, alpha diversity (Hill numbers), and beta diversity (TBI/B/C) w/o environment
#| echo: false

# 16.2 Fit GAMs for each response using only hurricane terms
gam_abun_hurr <- gam(Abundance_z ~ Months_Since_Hurricane,
                     data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")

gam_q0_hurr <- gam(Hill_q0_z ~ Months_Since_Hurricane,
                   data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")

gam_q1_hurr <- gam(Hill_q1_z ~ Months_Since_Hurricane,
                   data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")

gam_q2_hurr <- gam(Hill_q2_z ~ Months_Since_Hurricane,
                   data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")

gam_TBI_hurr <- gam(TBI_z ~ Months_Since_Hurricane,
                    data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")

gam_B_hurr <- gam(B_prop_z ~ Months_Since_Hurricane,
                  data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")

gam_C_hurr <- gam(C_prop_z ~ Months_Since_Hurricane,
                  data = sample_event_summary_std, method = "REML")


# 16.3 Function to extract hurricane effect sizes
extract_hurricane_effect_size <- function(gam_model, metric_name) {
  broom::tidy(gam_model, parametric = TRUE) %>%
    filter(grepl("Months_Since_Hurricane", term)) %>%
    mutate(
      Metric = metric_name,
      Term = recode(term,
                    "Months_Since_Hurricane1-3" = "Months 1-3",
                    "Months_Since_Hurricane4-5" = "Months 4-5"),
      EffectSize = abs(estimate / std.error),
      Significance = ifelse(p.value <= 0.05, "Significant", "Not significant")
    ) %>%
    select(Metric, Term, estimate, std.error, EffectSize, p.value, Significance)
}

# 16.4 Apply function to all hurricane-only GAMs
hurr_effect_q0   <- extract_hurricane_effect_size(gam_q0_hurr, "Richness (q=0)")
hurr_effect_q1   <- extract_hurricane_effect_size(gam_q1_hurr, "Hill-Shannon (q=1)")
hurr_effect_q2   <- extract_hurricane_effect_size(gam_q2_hurr, "Hill-Simpson (q=2)")
hurr_effect_TBI  <- extract_hurricane_effect_size(gam_TBI_hurr, "TBI")
hurr_effect_B    <- extract_hurricane_effect_size(gam_B_hurr, "Losses")
hurr_effect_C    <- extract_hurricane_effect_size(gam_C_hurr, "Gains")

# 16.5 Combine into a single table
hurricane_effects_only <- bind_rows( hurr_effect_q0, hurr_effect_q1, hurr_effect_q2,
  hurr_effect_TBI, hurr_effect_B, hurr_effect_C
) %>%
  arrange(Metric, Term)

# 16.6 Reorder Metric factor levels
hurricane_effects_only <- hurricane_effects_only %>%
  mutate(
    Metric = factor(Metric, levels = c(
      "Richness (q=0)",
      "Hill-Shannon (q=1)",
      "Hill-Simpson (q=2)",
      "TBI",
      "Losses",
      "Gains"
    ))
  ) %>%
  arrange(Metric, Term)

# 16.7 Make the plot
hurr1 <- ggplot(hurricane_effects_only, aes(x = Term, y = estimate)) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = Significance), size = 3, color="steelblue3") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("Not significant" = 15, "Significant" = 8)) +
  facet_wrap(~Metric, nrow = 2) +
  labs(
    x = "Term",
    y = "Effect size / EDF",
    shape = "Significance",
    title = "A"
  ) +
 theme_craig() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "grey20", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
  )



```

```{r}
#| label: Figure 6 Hurricane Effects
#| echo:  FALSE
#| message: FALSE
#| fig.cap: "<strong> Figure 6:</strong> Hurricane-driven and environmental effects on diversity metrics and their multivariate relationships. (A) GAM estimated effect sizes (EDF) of hurricane impacts on alpha diversity (Hill numbers q = 0, 1, 2), Total Beta Diversity (TBI), and taxonomic Gains and Losses, without accounting for environmental covariates. Asterisks indicate statistically significant responses. (B) GAM estimated effect sizes (EDF) from models that include environmental variables, showing how hurricane effects compare when abiotic drivers are incorporated. (C) Multidimensional scaling (MDS) ordination of correlations among diversity metrics, environmental variables, and hurricane timing."
#| fig.align: center
#| fig.width: 8
#| fig.height: 11

#madness

# Encode Months_Since_Hurricane as numeric for correlations
sample_event_summary_std <- sample_event_summary_std %>%
  mutate(Months_Num = case_when(
    Months_Since_Hurricane == "Pre" ~ 0,
    Months_Since_Hurricane == "1-3" ~ 1,
    Months_Since_Hurricane == "4-5" ~ 2
  ))

# Select variables
cor_vars <- sample_event_summary_std %>%
  select(
    Abundance_z, Hill_q0_z, Hill_q1_z, Hill_q2_z,
    TBI_z, B_prop_z, C_prop_z,
    mean_temp_C, mean_salinity_ppt, mean_DO_mg.L, mean_chlorophyll_ug.L,
    Months_Num
  )

#correlations
cor_matrix <- cor(cor_vars, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

## Convert correlation to distance
dist_matrix <- as.dist(1 - cor_matrix)  # 1 - correlation for distance

# Classical MDS
mds_res <- cmdscale(dist_matrix, k = 2, eig = TRUE)

mds_df <- data.frame(
  Dim1 = mds_res$points[,1],
  Dim2 = mds_res$points[,2],
  Variable = rownames(cor_matrix)
)


mds_df <- mds_df %>%
  mutate(
```
